title: America, the Once Global Nation
gist: Historian Odd Arne Westad argues the United States built its 250-year global position through mass immigration and cultural openness as much as military power, but is now rebelling against the very international order it created — a divergence crystallized by a GOP base sharply out of step with global opinion on climate and inequality, and by refugee and immigration policies widely read abroad as racially skewed.

insights.claim: The United States has taken in close to 85 million immigrants since 1870; roughly 51 million of its current population are immigrants, and nearly half of all Americans have at least one foreign-born grandparent — the demographic scale underpinning the country's claim to be 'the first empire that is also a global nation.'
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: Quantifies the demographic base of U.S. soft power that the piece argues is now being domestically undermined by immigration-restrictive politics.
insights.quote: the first empire that is also a global nation
insights.locator: The United States is, in many ways, the first empire

insights.claim: America's global appeal historically rested more on cultural and consumer openness — the promise that 'anyone could become an American' through consumption, culture, or migration — than on military or economic power alone, giving it an attraction the author says no other country has matched in modern times.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: Frames U.S. soft power as culturally rather than militarily rooted — relevant to assessing how much damage restrictive immigration/cultural politics does to long-run U.S. global influence versus hard-power metrics.
insights.quote: Anyone could become an American, whether through consumption, culture, or migration.
insights.locator: For the world at large, America’s openness

themes.theme: The cost and character of U.S. interventionism

insights.claim: World War I was the psychological turning point for U.S. global engagement — the first time American troops fought major powers abroad — and despite comparatively light U.S. casualties (just over 300,000, versus nearly 3 million British and over 7 million Russian dead), the domestic shock split the country between interventionists and anti-interventionists for almost a generation.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: Establishes a historical precedent for how disproportionate psychological reaction to relatively low U.S. casualties has repeatedly shaped U.S. willingness to engage abroad — a framework for reading today's war-fatigue politics.
insights.locator: World War I was a dividing line.

insights.claim: World War II and the Cold War (fought mainly in Korea and Vietnam) cost the U.S. roughly 1 million dead and wounded plus more than 300,000 more, respectively — the price that cemented Washington's role rebuilding Europe and Japan into a U.S.-led economic and political order and becoming the postwar hegemon.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: Quantifies the human cost underlying the postwar U.S.-led order now being questioned domestically — a baseline for assessing the relative price of maintaining versus abandoning that order.
insights.locator: The combination of World War II and the Cold War

insights.claim: Despite pushing other nations toward free trade for over a century — sometimes by force — the U.S. itself arguably did not practice genuine free trade until the Reagan administration in the 1980s, a policy inconsistency embedded in a century of U.S. economic diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: A specific historical inconsistency useful for framing current U.S. tariff/trade-policy debates as a continuation of a long pattern of asymmetric U.S. trade demands rather than a Trump-era novelty.
insights.quote: the United States itself arguably did not truly practice free trade until the Reagan administration in the 1980s.
insights.locator: Ironically, America only proved willing to open its own markets

insights.claim: U.S. foreign policy has instinctively opposed social revolution since viewing the French Revolution as the 'evil twin' of 1776, through opposing 20th-century socialism abroad — a reflexive anti-revolutionary stance that made Washington reluctant to back anti-colonial independence movements and, per the author, still tarnishes U.S. standing among the successors of post-colonial-era leaders today.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: Identifies a durable ideological blind spot (anti-revolutionary reflex) linked to continued reputational costs in the Global South — relevant to assessing U.S. soft-power deficits versus China in developing-world diplomacy.
insights.locator: At the end of the 1700s, many U.S. leaders saw the French Revolution

insights.claim: Fewer than one-third of Republican voters believe in human-driven climate change, electric vehicles, or lowering tariffs, and relatively few see social inequality as a problem, whereas globally an average of close to 80% of people view inequality as a 'very big' or 'moderately big' issue — a stark divergence between the current U.S. governing coalition and world opinion.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: A hard data point for gauging how far the current U.S. political coalition's priorities diverge from global public opinion — relevant to assessing durability of U.S. leadership on climate/ESG-linked multilateral initiatives.
insights.locator: Less than one-third of Republican voters believe

insights.claim: President Trump's December 2025 rally remarks reiterating his 2018 'shithole countries' comments, preferring immigrants from Norway, Sweden, and Denmark over Somalia, exemplify why the current administration is widely viewed abroad as unapologetically racist on immigration policy.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: Directly links a specific, dated presidential statement to the reputational-cost thesis of the piece — trackable rhetoric that investors in U.S. soft-power-dependent sectors (education, tourism, tech talent recruitment) should weigh.
insights.quote: Why is it we only take people from shithole countries[?] Why can’t we have some people from Norway, Sweden, just a few?

insights.claim: Between October 2025 and April 2026, 99% of refugees resettled in the U.S. came from South Africa — a country where whites control most national wealth — while only three refugees were admitted from Afghanistan despite a 20-year U.S. war there, illustrating a racially skewed refugee-prioritization policy.
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: A concrete, checkable statistic for quantifying the administration's refugee-policy priorities — useful for assessing diplomatic/reputational fallout with Afghan-allied and African governments.
insights.quote: Of the small number of refugees resettled in the United States between October 2025 and April 2026, 99 percent were from South Africa
insights.locator: Trump’s prioritization of white South Africans as refugees

insights.claim: With Trump's approval around 36% making a Democratic return to power increasingly likely, the author predicts the U.S. will eventually recover from its current internal division, but that the world will by then be fundamentally transformed toward greater economic diversity and great-power multipolarity — a world the U.S. itself brought about 'by denial and omission more than design.'
insights.speaker: Odd Arne Westad
insights.so_what: A forward-looking structural call: bet on continued great-power multipolarity emerging regardless of the outcome of U.S. domestic politics — a framework for long-horizon geopolitical-risk allocation.
insights.quote: by denial and omission more than design.
insights.locator: Ironically, this will also be a world
